| Modified Food Starch | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Category | Thickeners and stabilizers |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

**Modified food starches** are those starches") that have been deliberately treated to modify them and give them certain desirable characteristics for use in cooking.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) They stand in contrast to native starches.

## Source

[edit | edit source]

Various different native starches can be modified, including corn, potato, etc.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Waxy maize, however, is popular for modification due to its clarity, clean flavor, and lower tendency to congealing.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The precise characteristics of a modified starch will depend on the variety.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) In general, however, modified starches are formulated for increased stability, such as under heat, acidity, freezing, and mixing.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5) They can also vary in their speed and temperature of gelatinization,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) as well as their overall texture.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) There are several varieties of modified starch, and some common varieties are described below.

### Instant

[edit | edit source]

Native starches need to be hydrated and then heated to the gelatinization point in order to provide thickening power.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Instant starch (also called pregelatinized or cold-water-swelling starch)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) is made by gelatinizing the starch in advance, then cooling and drying it.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:6-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) After reconstitution with liquid by the cook, the mixture will thicken without needing further heating.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) Alternatively, other modifications that result in the same ability to thicken without heat can be used to make instant starch.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Instant Clearjel and Ultrasperse 2000 are two brand-name examples of this variety.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

This variety is particularly useful for thickening mixtures that would be damaged by heating, such those containing heat-sensitive fruits.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) It is also useful for last-minute thickening.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) However, the resulting texture will not be identical to that from native starches.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

### Acid-hydrolyzed

[edit | edit source]

This variety of starch, also called lintnerized, Naegeli, or acid‐thinned starch,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) is made making a slurry of the starch and an acidic solution before heating it below the gelatinization temperature.[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) It is then neutralized, washed, and dried.[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) This process degrades the starch molecules into smaller segments that retain only their crystalline and not amorphous character, and these smaller segments can associate closely together but disperse easily.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) As a result, the gelatinization temperature increases, the solubility of the starch increases, the viscosity of the pasted starch decreases, and the cooled gel increases in strength.[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8)

Acid-hydrolyzed starches are particularly useful for some gummy confections,[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) which rely on a low viscosity mixture when hot before setting to a firm and stable gel upon cooking.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

### Substituted

[edit | edit source]

Native starches will eventually retrograde after gelatinization, pasting, and cooling.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Substitution refers to a process that binds other chemicals to the starch molecules, making the starch molecules bulkier and less able to bind tightly together.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) This helps reduce starch retrogradation and its negative effects such as excessive gelling, increased opacity, and syneresis.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) This looser binding also lowers the temperature required to gelatinize the starch,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) and substituted starch is also more stable to freezing and thawing.[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7)

### Cross-linked

[edit | edit source]

This modified starch is made by forming bridges, or cross-links, between the starch molecules.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) This inhibits swelling and therefore makes the starch more resistant to gelatinization,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) and it reduces retrogradation.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) The texture is "short" and not gummy or stringy,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) and it is less vulnerable to shear thinning.[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) These starches are not suitable for low-temperature products.[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8)

### Stabilized

[edit | edit source]

Stabilized starches, sometimes called "dual-modified", are both substituted and cross-linked.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) These generally produce clear results that are fairly tolerant to acidity, retrogradation, and freeze-thawing.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8)

### Oxidized

[edit | edit source]

Oxidizing starches reduces their gelatinization temperature and pasting velocity.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) The resulting product is more fluid and less vulnerable to retrogradation,[[7]](#cite_note-:3-7) and the dry product is good at adhering to meat and fish.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

There is no single use for modified starch, since there are so many varieties with different properties. Generally, they confer stability,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) however it is desired. Heat-sensitive products benefit from instant starches.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## Recipes

[edit | edit source]

Category *Recipes using modified food starch* not found
